narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students like you, looking at factors such as early and later school grades, undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, family income, and whether you'd study full-time to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you specifically, the model estimated a 67% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for denial since the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers—far below the typical mid-range for most students. Your Year 1 decile, also in the lowest group and well under the average, added to this concern as another strong signal of academic struggle. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 36 stood strong, just slightly below the average of 36.7, and it notably pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, however, which is lower than the typical 3.21, contributed somewhat toward the failure side. Your lower-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, nudging the odds toward passing compared to higher-income averages, and planning to study full-time aligned with the vast majority of successful students, offering a small boost. Overall, your profile shows strengths in test performance and commitment, but the low school deciles outweighed them when compared to students who typically pass the bar. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school rankings, aiming for a higher undergraduate GPA if possible, and perhaps exploring resources tailored for Black women in law like mentorship networks or prep courses that address these gaps. You're capable of turning this around—many do with targeted improvements—and reapplying with stronger academics could change the outcome next time. Keep pushing; your journey matters.
